texts.text: Topchiev Institute of Petrochemical Synthesis, Russian Academy of Sciences, Moscow, 119991 Russia

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Abstract  Novel one-step technique has been proposed for octyl-substituted polymethylsiloxane (POMS) synthesis and vulcanization. The technique makes it possible to prepare С 3 /С1  selective gas separation membranes. The fact of POMS formation and vulcanization by hydrosilylation reaction involving 1-octene and polymethylhydrosiloxane has been confirmed by IR spectroscopy data. On the basis of the results obtained by measuring permeability of POMS membranes to permanent gases and propane, a preferred modifier to crosslinker ratio at which polymer films with the best gas transport properties can be obtained has been estimated  at  95/5.  Experimentally  determined  permeability  coefficients  for  methane  and  propane  (270  and 1560 barrer, respectively) and ideal propane/methane selectivity of 5.8 allow for the conclusion that POMS obtained in the study has transport properties comparable to those of similar membrane materials reported in the literature as obtained via multistep synthesis.
texts.text: Abstract  Novel one-step technique has been proposed for octyl-substituted polymethylsiloxane (POMS) synthesis and vulcanization. The technique makes it possible to prepare С 3 /С1  selective gas separation membranes. The fact of POMS formation and vulcanization by hydrosilylation reaction involving 1-octene and polymethylhydrosiloxane has been confirmed by IR spectroscopy data. On the basis of the results obtained by measuring permeability of POMS membranes to permanent gases and propane, a preferred modifier to crosslinker ratio at which polymer films with the best gas transport properties can be obtained has been estimated  at  95/5.  Experimentally  determined  permeability  coefficients  for  methane  and  propane  (270  and 1560 barrer, respectively) and ideal propane/methane selectivity of 5.8 allow for the conclusion that POMS obtained in the study has transport properties comparable to those of similar membrane materials reported in the literature as obtained via multistep synthesis.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Keywords: polyoctylmethylsiloxane,  polymethylhydrosiloxane,  1,7-octadiene,  hydrosilylation,  siloxane modification, vulcanization, separation of hydrocarbons, membrane gas separation, POMS
texts.text: Keywords: polyoctylmethylsiloxane,  polymethylhydrosiloxane,  1,7-octadiene,  hydrosilylation,  siloxane modification, vulcanization, separation of hydrocarbons, membrane gas separation, POMS

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In  the  Russian  energy  source  market,  there  is  a growing  demand  for  gas  fuel:  gas  consumption  was 459 bln  cubic  meters  in  2010  and  is  estimated  to increase to 528 bln cubic meters by the year of 2025, with  the  proportion  of  multicomponent  gas  that requires additional conversion being expected to increase from 66% in 2010 to 94-95% by the year of 2040 [1]. Gas conditioning comprises the removal of carbon dioxide, water, nitrogen, and hydrogen sulfide using adsorption columns, scrubbers, or cooling technologies  (cryogenic  distillation).  These  technologies are also used for separation and concentration of valuable  condensable  hydrocarbons  including  propane and butane. Membrane gas separation is a promising technique for solving the problem [2-4]. Membrane gas separation modules are capable of competing with conventional  technologies  due  to  a  smaller  size  and the fact that they use intrinsic gas stream pressure as a driving force. Gas separation membranes are already used for industrial natural gas treatment, air separation, and hydrogen recovery; nonetheless, gas separation  issues  are  still  extensively  studied  by  numerous international groups [5-8].
texts.text: In  the  Russian  energy  source  market,  there  is  a growing  demand  for  gas  fuel:  gas  consumption  was 459 bln  cubic  meters  in  2010  and  is  estimated  to increase to 528 bln cubic meters by the year of 2025, with  the  proportion  of  multicomponent  gas  that requires additional conversion being expected to increase from 66% in 2010 to 94-95% by the year of 2040 [1]. Gas conditioning comprises the removal of carbon dioxide, water, nitrogen, and hydrogen sulfide using adsorption columns, scrubbers, or cooling technologies  (cryogenic  distillation).  These  technologies are also used for separation and concentration of valuable  condensable  hydrocarbons  including  propane and butane. Membrane gas separation is a promising technique for solving the problem [2-4]. Membrane gas separation modules are capable of competing with conventional  technologies  due  to  a  smaller  size  and the fact that they use intrinsic gas stream pressure as a driving force. Gas separation membranes are already used for industrial natural gas treatment, air separation, and hydrogen recovery; nonetheless, gas separation  issues  are  still  extensively  studied  by  numerous international groups [5-8].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: pane  and  butane,  from  natural  gas.  An  important aspect in the choice of membrane material and separation conditions is that С 3+ hydrocarbons in dry natural  gas  may  make  up  to  15%  [9].  This  implies  the necessity for choosing the material capable of selective isolation of the hydrocarbons from methane mixtures.
texts.text: pane  and  butane,  from  natural  gas.  An  important aspect in the choice of membrane material and separation conditions is that С 3+ hydrocarbons in dry natural  gas  may  make  up  to  15%  [9].  This  implies  the necessity for choosing the material capable of selective isolation of the hydrocarbons from methane mixtures.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Selective vapor transport should be due to the thermodynamic component; i.e., the solubility coefficient of organic vapor in question (propane) is to be higher than  that  of  methane.  The  requirements  are  met  by polymer  materials,  such  as  glassy  polymers  having high fractional free volume [10-13] and silicone rubbers having a low glass transition temperature and high chain mobility [13-22]. Prominent representatives of membranes based on silicone rubbers are polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) or polyoctylmethylsiloxane (POMS)  membranes,  which  are  used  in  industrial membrane  gas  separation  modules  [7,  15,  23].  For example, Borsig, SiHi, and Dalian Eurofilm produce membrane  separation  units  using  composite  membranes based on silicone rubbers [5]. The main disadvantage of silicone rubbers is poor film-forming ability, which can be overcome by chemical crosslinking of the polymers.
texts.text: Selective vapor transport should be due to the thermodynamic component; i.e., the solubility coefficient of organic vapor in question (propane) is to be higher than  that  of  methane.  The  requirements  are  met  by polymer  materials,  such  as  glassy  polymers  having high fractional free volume [10-13] and silicone rubbers having a low glass transition temperature and high chain mobility [13-22]. Prominent representatives of membranes based on silicone rubbers are polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) or polyoctylmethylsiloxane (POMS)  membranes,  which  are  used  in  industrial membrane  gas  separation  modules  [7,  15,  23].  For example, Borsig, SiHi, and Dalian Eurofilm produce membrane  separation  units  using  composite  membranes based on silicone rubbers [5]. The main disadvantage of silicone rubbers is poor film-forming ability, which can be overcome by chemical crosslinking of the polymers.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  approach  proposed  in  [16,  17]  to  fabricate membranes from crosslinked POMS involves a reaction  between  remaining Si-H bonds of linear functionalized siloxane and a crosslinking agent (disilanol) in the presence of tetraethoxyor tetramethoxysilane in  toluene.  The  crosslinking  catalyst  was  dibutyltin dilaurate.
texts.text: The  approach  proposed  in  [16,  17]  to  fabricate membranes from crosslinked POMS involves a reaction  between  remaining Si-H bonds of linear functionalized siloxane and a crosslinking agent (disilanol) in the presence of tetraethoxyor tetramethoxysilane in  toluene.  The  crosslinking  catalyst  was  dibutyltin dilaurate.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the present work, we propose a novel one-step technique  for  preparing  and  vulcanizing  modified polymethylsiloxanes using POMS as an example. The technique involves in situ modification and crosslinking,  i.e.  without  the  product  isolation  step  between modification and crosslinking. Figure 2 [17, 26] collates  the  features  of  this  technique  and  those  of  the aforementioned  methods  for  fabricating  membranes based on modified polymethylsiloxanes.
texts.text: In the present work, we propose a novel one-step technique  for  preparing  and  vulcanizing  modified polymethylsiloxanes using POMS as an example. The technique involves in situ modification and crosslinking,  i.e.  without  the  product  isolation  step  between modification and crosslinking. Figure 2 [17, 26] collates  the  features  of  this  technique  and  those  of  the aforementioned  methods  for  fabricating  membranes based on modified polymethylsiloxanes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The objective of this work is to develop a one-step procedure for synthesizing and vulcanizing membrane materials based on modified polymethylsiloxanes having  elevated  sorption  selectivity  and  permeability  of organic vapors relative to those of permanent gases.
texts.text: The objective of this work is to develop a one-step procedure for synthesizing and vulcanizing membrane materials based on modified polymethylsiloxanes having  elevated  sorption  selectivity  and  permeability  of organic vapors relative to those of permanent gases.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: POMS membranes were synthesized via the hydrosilylation  reaction  (Fig.  3)  using  polymethylhydrosiloxane,  1-octene,  and  1,7-octadiene  over  Karstedt's catalyst. In the present work, the following chemicals were used: polymethylhydrosiloxane ( Mn 1700-3200, Aldrich) dissolved in hexane (reagent grade), 1-octene (Aldrich), 1,7-octadiene (Aldrich), Karstedt's catalyst
texts.text: POMS membranes were synthesized via the hydrosilylation  reaction  (Fig.  3)  using  polymethylhydrosiloxane,  1-octene,  and  1,7-octadiene  over  Karstedt's catalyst. In the present work, the following chemicals were used: polymethylhydrosiloxane ( Mn 1700-3200, Aldrich) dissolved in hexane (reagent grade), 1-octene (Aldrich), 1,7-octadiene (Aldrich), Karstedt's catalyst

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A 3 wt % polymethylhydrosiloxane (PMHS) solution  in  hexane  was  mixed  with  1-alkene  (modifier), 30 µ L of Karstedt's catalyst was added, and the mixture was stirred at 60 ° C for 2 hours. In order to prevent solvent loss by evaporation, a water-cooled reflux condenser  was  used.  Then,  1,7-octadiene  (crosslinking agent) was added, the amount of PMHS was brought to the stoichiometric ratio, and the resulting mixture was stirred at 60 ° C for 1 h. The membrane was fabricated by casting onto mesh (cell size 40 µ m), mounted on  Teflon  film,  and  subsequent  drying  at  60 ° C  for 24 hours. The special features of the method are the absence of the product separation step between modification and crosslinking and the use of the same catalyst for both the reactions. The degree of substitution of Si-C for Si-H bonds was determined by calculating  the  specimen  mass  balance.  Notation  for  membranes obtained is given in Table 1.
texts.text: A 3 wt % polymethylhydrosiloxane (PMHS) solution  in  hexane  was  mixed  with  1-alkene  (modifier), 30 µ L of Karstedt's catalyst was added, and the mixture was stirred at 60 ° C for 2 hours. In order to prevent solvent loss by evaporation, a water-cooled reflux condenser  was  used.  Then,  1,7-octadiene  (crosslinking agent) was added, the amount of PMHS was brought to the stoichiometric ratio, and the resulting mixture was stirred at 60 ° C for 1 h. The membrane was fabricated by casting onto mesh (cell size 40 µ m), mounted on  Teflon  film,  and  subsequent  drying  at  60 ° C  for 24 hours. The special features of the method are the absence of the product separation step between modification and crosslinking and the use of the same catalyst for both the reactions. The degree of substitution of Si-C for Si-H bonds was determined by calculating  the  specimen  mass  balance.  Notation  for  membranes obtained is given in Table 1.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Infrared spectra of the films were recorded in the reflection mode using a HYPERION-2000 IR microscope coupled with Bruker IFS 66 v/s Fourier-transform IR spectrometer (Ge crystal, 50 scans, resolution 2 cm -1 , range 600-4000 cm -1 ).
texts.text: Infrared spectra of the films were recorded in the reflection mode using a HYPERION-2000 IR microscope coupled with Bruker IFS 66 v/s Fourier-transform IR spectrometer (Ge crystal, 50 scans, resolution 2 cm -1 , range 600-4000 cm -1 ).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  membranes  gas  permeability  and  diffusion coefficients for nitrogen, methane, and propane were measured  at  30 ° С  according  to  the  Daynes-Barrer technique  using  a  Helmholtz-Zentrum  Geesthacht precision  unit  with  a  pressure  transducer  (Baratron) accurate  to  10 -7   bar.  The  permeability  coefficient  is given in barrers. Sorption coefficient was calculated as the  ratio  of  the  permeability  to  the  diffusion  coefficient. The gas permeability of membranes was determined in the same way in [27].
texts.text: The  membranes  gas  permeability  and  diffusion coefficients for nitrogen, methane, and propane were measured  at  30 ° С  according  to  the  Daynes-Barrer technique  using  a  Helmholtz-Zentrum  Geesthacht precision  unit  with  a  pressure  transducer  (Baratron) accurate  to  10 -7   bar.  The  permeability  coefficient  is given in barrers. Sorption coefficient was calculated as the  ratio  of  the  permeability  to  the  diffusion  coefficient. The gas permeability of membranes was determined in the same way in [27].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Characterization of Modified PMHS by IR Spectroscopy
texts.text: Characterization of Modified PMHS by IR Spectroscopy

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Figures 4 and 5 give IR survey spectra for all the POMS  membranes  of  different  compositions  (1,7octadiene/1-octene ratio = 100/0-5/95) in comparison with the spectrum of pure (unmodified) PMHS. The  IR  spectra  of  all  POMS  specimens  show  the almost complete disappearance of the intense band at 2165 cm -1  due to Si-H bonds, thereby indicating the occurrence of the hydrosilylation reaction. New, quite intense bands due to stretching (2924 and 2857 cm -1 ) and bending (1461 cm -1 ) vibrations of -СН 2- groups appear,  and  the  intensity  and  the  position  of  maximums for bands in the 700-1050 cm -1  range (Si-C and  Si-O  bonds)  drastically  change,  indicating  the addition  of  a  long  hydrocarbon  fragment  (С 8 Н17)  to PMHS. The data show that PMHS crosslinking takes place  in  all  the  samples,  being  accompanied  by the disappearance of Si-H bonds and introduction of -СН2-chains in the polymer composition.
texts.text: Figures 4 and 5 give IR survey spectra for all the POMS  membranes  of  different  compositions  (1,7octadiene/1-octene ratio = 100/0-5/95) in comparison with the spectrum of pure (unmodified) PMHS. The  IR  spectra  of  all  POMS  specimens  show  the almost complete disappearance of the intense band at 2165 cm -1  due to Si-H bonds, thereby indicating the occurrence of the hydrosilylation reaction. New, quite intense bands due to stretching (2924 and 2857 cm -1 ) and bending (1461 cm -1 ) vibrations of -СН 2- groups appear,  and  the  intensity  and  the  position  of  maximums for bands in the 700-1050 cm -1  range (Si-C and  Si-O  bonds)  drastically  change,  indicating  the addition  of  a  long  hydrocarbon  fragment  (С 8 Н17)  to PMHS. The data show that PMHS crosslinking takes place  in  all  the  samples,  being  accompanied  by the disappearance of Si-H bonds and introduction of -СН2-chains in the polymer composition.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Figure  4  shows  that  the  2165-cm -1   band  due  to Si-H  bonds  is  totally  absent  from  the  spectra  of POMS 100/0 ( 1 ), 20/80 ( 2 ) and 5/95 ( 5 ) specimens. A trace amount (<1%) of such bonds remains in POMS 40/60  ( 3 ),  and  a  even  smaller  amount  is  present  in POMS 5/95' ( 4 ). So, it can be concluded that heating is  necessary  for  the  synthesis  in  order  to  obtain  a

A trace amount (<1%) of such bonds remains in POMS 40/60  ( 3 ),  and  a  even  smaller  amount  is  present  in POMS 5/95' ( 4 ). So, it can be concluded that heating is  necessary  for  the  synthesis  in  order  to  obtain  a

texts.label: text
texts.orig: higher  degree  of  substitution  and  that  compositions having  the  1,7-octadiene/1-octene  ratio  more  than 40/60 do not provide a high substitution degree.
texts.text: higher  degree  of  substitution  and  that  compositions having  the  1,7-octadiene/1-octene  ratio  more  than 40/60 do not provide a high substitution degree.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As can be seen from Fig. 6, the spectrum of POMS 100/0 exhibits a weak C=C band (1655 cm -1 ), a weak band at 910 cm -1 , and a band of medium intensity at 960 cm -1  due to CH=CH bending vibrations in 1-olefins. So, it can be concluded that 1,7-octadiene may add to the siloxane Si-H bond not only through both the vinyl groups but also through one (octenyl substituent  addition,  not  crosslinking),  with  the  terminal double bond being able to migrate inside by the hydrocarbon chain. Such double-bond migration induced by a Pt-containing catalyst is well known and, in particular it has been studied in the siloxane hydrosilylation  reaction,  as  detailed  in  [24].  When  octene-1  is introduced into the reaction mixture, the intensity of bands corresponding to octenyl substituents decreases. Furthermore,  at  an  octene-1  concentration  higher than 80%, there is the complete fixation of 1,7-octadiene  by  both  the  vinyl  groups  (POMS  20/80  and POMS 5/95 specimens); the bands due to both double bonds and Si-H bonds almost completely disappear from the spectra of the specimens. The increase in the proportion of olefinic bonds in the specimen with a decrease in the octene-1 concentration is presumably due  to  steriс  hindrance  that  occurs  between  PMHS macromolecules as a result of crosslinking.  An increase  in  the  proportion  of  olefinic  bonds  is  also observed  in  POMS  5/95'  specimen,  although  the spectrum of a thermally conditioned specimen of the same composition (POMS 5/95) shows almost complete absence of double bonds. The absence of double bonds  in  the  POMS  5/95  specimen  suggests  the occurrence of the exhaustive crosslinking reaction.
texts.text: As can be seen from Fig. 6, the spectrum of POMS 100/0 exhibits a weak C=C band (1655 cm -1 ), a weak band at 910 cm -1 , and a band of medium intensity at 960 cm -1  due to CH=CH bending vibrations in 1-olefins. So, it can be concluded that 1,7-octadiene may add to the siloxane Si-H bond not only through both the vinyl groups but also through one (octenyl substituent  addition,  not  crosslinking),  with  the  terminal double bond being able to migrate inside by the hydrocarbon chain. Such double-bond migration induced by a Pt-containing catalyst is well known and, in particular it has been studied in the siloxane hydrosilylation  reaction,  as  detailed  in  [24].  When  octene-1  is introduced into the reaction mixture, the intensity of bands corresponding to octenyl substituents decreases. Furthermore,  at  an  octene-1  concentration  higher than 80%, there is the complete fixation of 1,7-octadiene  by  both  the  vinyl  groups  (POMS  20/80  and POMS 5/95 specimens); the bands due to both double bonds and Si-H bonds almost completely disappear from the spectra of the specimens. The increase in the proportion of olefinic bonds in the specimen with a decrease in the octene-1 concentration is presumably due  to  steriс  hindrance  that  occurs  between  PMHS macromolecules as a result of crosslinking.  An increase  in  the  proportion  of  olefinic  bonds  is  also observed  in  POMS  5/95'  specimen,  although  the spectrum of a thermally conditioned specimen of the same composition (POMS 5/95) shows almost complete absence of double bonds. The absence of double bonds  in  the  POMS  5/95  specimen  suggests  the occurrence of the exhaustive crosslinking reaction.

To  assess  the effect  of  the  1,7-octadiene/1-octene  ratio  on  the degree of completion of the hydrosilylation reaction, the degree of substitution of Si-C for Si-H bonds was determined. texts.label: text
texts.orig: The data in Table 2 show that as the 1,7-octadiene/1-octene ratio decreases (proportion of octene-1 fraction in the reactant mixture increases), the degree of  substitution  of  Si-C  for  Si-H  bonds  grows.  The maximal  degree  of  replacement  of  Si-H  by  Si-C bonds within the examined range of the 1,7-octadiene/1-octene ratio was obtained for POMS 5/95.

The maximal  degree  of  replacement  of  Si-H  by  Si-C bonds within the examined range of the 1,7-octadiene/1-octene ratio was obtained for POMS 5/95.

The gas transport properties  were  measured  at  0.4  bar  and 30 ° C. Figures 7 and 8 present measurement results for

The gas solubility coefficient in the polymer is almost unaffected by changing the amount of the  crosslinker:  for  all  the  POMS  membranes, the nitrogen, methane and propane solubility coefficients are at the levels of 0.15, 0.70, and 12.20 × 10 -2 cm 3 /(cm 3  cmHg), respectively.
texts.text: permeability  and  diffusion  coefficients,  respectively, as well as ideal permeability selectivity values for gaseous N2, СН4, and С 3 Н8. The gas solubility coefficient in the polymer is almost unaffected by changing the amount of the  crosslinker:  for  all  the  POMS  membranes, the nitrogen, methane and propane solubility coefficients are at the levels of 0.15, 0.70, and 12.20 × 10 -2 cm 3 /(cm 3  cmHg), respectively.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: An increase in the crosslinker (1,7-octadiene) content in the initial mixture leads to a decrease in the permeability coefficient. The decrease is associated with a reduction in POMS chain mobility. The same phenomenon was observed for PDMS in the case of adding various amounts of tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) as a crosslinking  agent:  as  the  TEOS  mass  fraction  was increased from 0 to 30%, the N2 permeability decreased  from  639.69  to  184.91  barrer,  respectively [22].  Another  research  group  [28]  also  mentioned  a decrease on the PDMS membrane permeability coefficient with an increase in crosslink density: as PDMS crosslinking  reaction  temperature  rises  from  75  to 100 ° C, the polymer fractional free volume decreases, the СН4 permeability coefficient declines from 1000 to 480 barrer, СО 2 /СН4 separation selectivity decreases, and  the  crosslink  density  increases.  The  published data are in good agreement with the data obtained in this  study:  as  the  amount  of  1,7-octadiene  (crosslinker) increases, both the permeability coefficient for all the gases and the С 3 Н8/СН4 separation selectivity decrease. Based on comparison with data available in the literature, we can conclude that this effect is due to the  increase  in  the  polymer  crosslink  density.  An increase  in  the  degree  of  crosslinking  leads  to  a decrease in polymer fractional free volume. A decrease in polymer fractional free volume results in hindering gas  diffusion  through  the  membrane  material,  since
texts.text: An increase in the crosslinker (1,7-octadiene) content in the initial mixture leads to a decrease in the permeability coefficient. The decrease is associated with a reduction in POMS chain mobility. The same phenomenon was observed for PDMS in the case of adding various amounts of tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) as a crosslinking  agent:  as  the  TEOS  mass  fraction  was increased from 0 to 30%, the N2 permeability decreased  from  639.69  to  184.91  barrer,  respectively [22].  Another  research  group  [28]  also  mentioned  a decrease on the PDMS membrane permeability coefficient with an increase in crosslink density: as PDMS crosslinking  reaction  temperature  rises  from  75  to 100 ° C, the polymer fractional free volume decreases, the СН4 permeability coefficient declines from 1000 to 480 barrer, СО 2 /СН4 separation selectivity decreases, and  the  crosslink  density  increases.  The  published data are in good agreement with the data obtained in this  study:  as  the  amount  of  1,7-octadiene  (crosslinker) increases, both the permeability coefficient for all the gases and the С 3 Н8/СН4 separation selectivity decrease. Based on comparison with data available in the literature, we can conclude that this effect is due to the  increase  in  the  polymer  crosslink  density.  An increase  in  the  degree  of  crosslinking  leads  to  a decrease in polymer fractional free volume. A decrease in polymer fractional free volume results in hindering gas  diffusion  through  the  membrane  material,  since

texts.orig: the transport of gas molecules in the polymer matrix is impeded.  The  solubility  coefficient  of  gases  in  the polymer  is  almost  unaffected  by  varying  the  crosslinker content. Thus, significant changes in gas permeability  and  permselectivity  are  due  the  change  in the diffusional component. According to the experimental  data  on  the  gas  transport  properties  of  the material within the 1,7-octadiene/1-octene ratio range  studied,  the  POMS  5/95  specimen  has  maximum selectivity and permeability values.

texts.orig: Table 3 collates methane and propane permeability data for the POMS 5/95 membrane having optimized composition and the relevant published data. It can be seen that permeability and separation selectivity coefficients are in good agreement with the data reported in [14].
texts.text: Table 3 collates methane and propane permeability data for the POMS 5/95 membrane having optimized composition and the relevant published data. It can be seen that permeability and separation selectivity coefficients are in good agreement with the data reported in [14].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In summary, the POMS membrane material prepared  via  the  novel  one-step  technique  ranks  by  its transport  properties  among  octyl-substituted  polymethylsiloxanes  obtained  by  other  research  groups. Thus,  the  new  method  for  synthesizing  substituted polymethylsiloxanes can be used to fabricate selective membrane materials.
texts.text: In summary, the POMS membrane material prepared  via  the  novel  one-step  technique  ranks  by  its transport  properties  among  octyl-substituted  polymethylsiloxanes  obtained  by  other  research  groups. Thus,  the  new  method  for  synthesizing  substituted polymethylsiloxanes can be used to fabricate selective membrane materials.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A  novel  one-step  approach  to  obtain  modified polymethylsiloxanes has been proposed. IR spectroscopy and mass balance calculation data show the technique to be efficient for obtaining membrane materials: Si-C bonds are substituted for Si-H bonds, the degree substitution being higher with a greater amount of the olefin introduced for modification, and a ; networked structure is formed. A preferred crosslinker to modifier ratio in the reaction mixture has been determined to be 5/95. At this ratio, the highest value of the permeability coefficient among the POMS specimens is obtained, as well as the maximum permeselectivity of 5.8 for the С 3 Н8/СН4 gas pair, in agreement with the data obtained by other scientific groups. So, the proposed one-step technique for synthesizing and vulcanizing polymethylsiloxanes  make  it  possible to obtain membrane material capable of selective separation of hydrocarbons.
texts.text: A  novel  one-step  approach  to  obtain  modified polymethylsiloxanes has been proposed. IR spectroscopy and mass balance calculation data show the technique to be efficient for obtaining membrane materials: Si-C bonds are substituted for Si-H bonds, the degree substitution being higher with a greater amount of the olefin introduced for modification, and a ; networked structure is formed. A preferred crosslinker to modifier ratio in the reaction mixture has been determined to be 5/95. At this ratio, the highest value of the permeability coefficient among the POMS specimens is obtained, as well as the maximum permeselectivity of 5.8 for the С 3 Н8/СН4 gas pair, in agreement with the data obtained by other scientific groups. So, the proposed one-step technique for synthesizing and vulcanizing polymethylsiloxanes  make  it  possible to obtain membrane material capable of selective separation of hydrocarbons.
